data.title: Does AIT’s Q1 Earnings Beat and Industrial Uptick Change The Bull Case For Applied Industrial Technologies (AIT)?
data.description:
• Applied Industrial Technologies recently posted first-quarter fiscal 2026 results that exceeded earnings and revenue expectations, supported by strength across core industrial distribution and engineered solutions operations.
  • This outperformance, reinforced by favorable analyst earnings estimate revisions and commentary about improving industrial sector conditions, has reinforced investor attention on the company’s execution and market positioning.
  • Against this backdrop, we’ll explore how the earnings beat and signs of industrial sector recovery could influence Applied Industrial Technologies’ investment narrative.

The best AI stocks today may lie beyond giants like Nvidia and Microsoft. Find the next big opportunity with these 23 smaller AI-focused companies with strong growth potential through early-stage innovation in machine learning, automation, and data intelligence that could fund your retirement.

Advertisement

What Is Applied Industrial Technologies' Investment Narrative?

To own Applied Industrial Technologies, you have to believe in a steady, execution-led story rather than a blockbuster growth one. The recent earnings beat and new high in the share price validate the company’s focus on high-quality industrial distribution and engineered solutions, but they also arguably pull some of the easy short term upside forward. Near term catalysts now center on whether upcoming quarters can sustain the raised fiscal 2026 guidance and keep earnings revisions moving in the right direction, especially if industrial sector recovery proves uneven. At the same time, the stock’s richer valuation multiples and modest forecast growth leave less room for disappointment if orders soften or margins come under pressure. In that sense, the latest results improve the narrative, but also raise the execution bar.

However, investors should be aware of the higher valuation risk if growth expectations cool. Applied Industrial Technologies' share price has been on the slide but might be up to 16% below fair value. Find out if it's a bargain.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Four Simply Wall St Community fair value views span roughly US$214 to US$303 per share, underscoring how far apart individual estimates can be. Set against a stock already above some of these views and priced for continued solid execution, the next few quarters of industrial demand and margin performance may matter even more for how this story unfolds.

data.title: Does Raised Q4 Guidance and A&D Strength Change the Bull Case For nLIGHT (LASR)?
data.description:
• Earlier in January 2026, nLIGHT, Inc. raised its fourth-quarter 2025 revenue guidance to US$78 million–US$80 million, citing stronger-than-expected Aerospace & Defense demand, and highlighted this outlook during its presentation at the 28th Annual Needham Growth Conference.
  • This combination of upgraded guidance and conference visibility underscores how nLIGHT’s Aerospace & Defense exposure is becoming a more important driver within its business mix.
  • We’ll now examine how the upgraded revenue guidance, particularly the Aerospace & Defense strength, shapes nLIGHT’s broader investment narrative.

Outshine the giants: these 23 early-stage AI stocks could fund your retirement.

Advertisement

What Is nLIGHT's Investment Narrative?

To own nLIGHT today, you have to believe its exposure to Aerospace & Defense can increasingly support a still-unprofitable, higher-multiple business that has already delivered a very large one-year share price gain. The raised Q4 2025 revenue guidance to US$78 million–US$80 million, driven by stronger Aerospace & Defense demand, reinforces that this segment is doing the heavy lifting in the near term and could remain a key catalyst as investors watch whether higher revenue begins to narrow losses. At the same time, nLIGHT still trades on a rich sales multiple, has a history of cash burn and margin pressure, and is not forecast to reach profitability over the next three years, which keeps execution risk front and center. The recent guidance upgrade helps, but it does not remove those concerns.

However, investors should also be aware of how nLIGHT’s rich valuation amplifies any execution missteps. Our comprehensive valuation report raises the possibility that nLIGHT is priced higher than what may be justified by its financials.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Two fair value estimates from the Simply Wall St Community span roughly US$22.83 to US$41.86, showing how widely individual views can differ. Set that against nLIGHT’s reliance on Aerospace & Defense strength as a key short term driver, and you can see why many readers may want to compare multiple perspectives before forming a view on the company’s prospects.

data.title: Is Safran’s eVTOL and Sustainable Propulsion Push Altering The Investment Case For Safran (ENXTPA:SAF)?
data.description:
• Recent commentary on Safran has highlighted rising opportunities in advanced aerospace and defense technologies, including eVTOL systems, long-range missiles, UAVs, and sustainable propulsion solutions, alongside expansion efforts in Asia Pacific and South America.
  • This clustering of growth themes across next-generation aircraft, non-air breathing systems, and government-focused programs underlines how broadly Safran is positioned across emerging aviation and defense niches.
  • We’ll now examine how Safran’s push into eVTOL and sustainable propulsion could shape its investment narrative in light of recent performance.

What Is Safran's Investment Narrative?

To own Safran, you need to believe it can keep turning its broad aerospace and defense footprint into resilient cash generation, while selectively extending into newer profit pools. The latest focus on eVTOLs, long-range missiles, UAVs and sustainable propulsion reinforces that Safran is leaning into segments where its existing propulsion and systems expertise should matter, but the near-term share price still looks tied more to civil aftermarket trends, execution on its raised 2025 guidance and capital returns like dividends and buybacks. Given the share price has moved only modestly lately, the market does not seem to treat this innovation push as a major short-term catalyst yet, though it could gradually reshape perceptions of Safran’s growth mix and risk profile, particularly its exposure to government programs and certification timelines.

However, the path to monetising these new technologies carries execution and timing risks investors should not overlook. Safran's share price has been on the slide but might be up to 6% below fair value. Find out if it's a bargain.

data.title: Is Tesla’s Robotaxi Push And FSD Subscriptions Altering The Investment Case For Tesla (TSLA)?
data.description:
• Tesla has recently begun offering unsupervised robotaxi rides in Austin and is phasing out one-time purchases of its Full Self-Driving software in favor of a monthly subscription model, while also rebranding its driver-assistance features amid regulatory scrutiny.
  • These moves signal Tesla’s push to reposition itself from a pure EV manufacturer toward a software, autonomy, and robotics platform, even as regulators and rivals closely examine its safety and marketing claims.
  • We’ll now examine how Tesla’s launch of unsupervised robotaxis could reshape the company’s investment narrative around recurring software-led revenues.

We've found 13 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

What Is Tesla's Investment Narrative?

Tesla is increasingly a conviction story about software, autonomy and robotics rather than pure EV volumes, which helps explain why the market still gives it a premium multiple despite lower margins, slowing deliveries and a low 6.4% return on equity. To stay invested, you really have to believe that Full Self-Driving, robotaxis in places like Austin and, longer term, Optimus can justify paying far above traditional auto valuations. The launch of unsupervised robotaxi rides and the shift to FSD subscriptions feed directly into that thesis, potentially strengthening the near term narrative around recurring, software-led revenue at a time when Q4 earnings are expected to show pressure on automotive profits. At the same time, the California license suspension, Autopilot rebrand and ongoing safety and DOJ scrutiny keep regulatory and execution risk very much front and center.

However, this premium story also leans heavily on regulators ultimately agreeing with Tesla’s view of autonomy. Tesla's shares are on the way up, but could they be overextended? Uncover how much higher they are than fair value.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Tesla’s 206 fair value estimates from the Simply Wall St Community span roughly US$70 to well over US$2,700, reflecting wildly different views on what autonomy and robotics are worth. That spread sits against a business where unsupervised robotaxis, regulatory pushback and compressed margins could all reshape how quickly software-led earnings actually show up. Together, these perspectives invite you to weigh both the bold upside story and the very real execution risks.

data.title: Does DFIN’s Q3 Beat and Software Pivot Reshape the Bull Case For Donnelley Financial Solutions (DFIN)?
data.description:
• Donnelley Financial Solutions recently reported Q3 2025 results that surpassed earnings expectations and outlined plans to increase the share of revenue from software solutions to 60% by 2028, while attracting new investment from the RAM Smid Composite fund.
  • This pivot toward higher-margin software and technology-enabled compliance tools marks a meaningful shift in how the company positions itself within the regulatory and financial reporting ecosystem.
  • We will now examine how Donnelley Financial Solutions’ push toward software-driven revenue reshapes its investment narrative for investors and stakeholders.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 22 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

What Is Donnelley Financial Solutions' Investment Narrative?

For Donnelley Financial Solutions, the big picture you need to buy into is a company trying to evolve from a cyclical services provider into a software-led compliance and transaction platform. The latest Q3 2025 beat on non GAAP EPS and the renewed push to reach 60% software revenue by 2028 give that story more credibility, especially alongside the RAM Smid Composite fund taking a new stake. In the near term, the key catalyst remains whether newer offerings like Active Intelligence, the revamped Venue data room, and EDGAR Next tools can offset what has been a 3% annual sales decline over the last five years and support margins after a large one off loss. The biggest risks are execution and pricing: the shift to higher margin software is not yet translating into faster top line growth, and the stock is already trading on a rich earnings multiple with low current ROE and compressed profit margins. If the software transition stalls or end markets stay sluggish, today’s valuation leaves limited room for error, despite the recent positive trading signals and institutional interest.

However, one risk in particular deserves closer attention from anyone considering the stock. Donnelley Financial Solutions' share price has been on the slide but might be dropping deeper into value territory. Find out whether it's a bargain at this price.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Community members on Simply Wall St have fair value estimates ranging from US$57.16 to US$64.33 across 2 views, which brackets the current consensus target and hints at only modest upside from here. Set against DFIN’s premium earnings multiple and still pressured margins, this spread underlines how differently investors can weigh the software pivot versus recent profit volatility, and why it pays to compare several viewpoints before deciding where you stand.

In the short term, the key catalysts still look like execution against 2025 guidance, continued share repurchases under the new US$100 million program, and any shifts in enrollment or pricing that filter quickly into earnings. The upbeat commentary may slightly ease concerns around valuation after the recent pullback, but it does little to reduce the core risks: regulatory scrutiny on for‑profit education, slower forecast growth than the wider market, and reliance on disciplined capital allocation.

data.title: Should Rapiblyk’s Expanded Distribution Through Cardinal Health Signal a Deeper Shift in CAH’s Specialty Strategy?
data.description:
• In January 2026, AOP Health US, LLC announced that its critical care beta-blocker Rapiblyk (landiolol) became more widely available nationwide through Cardinal Health and other major distributors, giving hospitals broader access via their preferred purchasing channels.
  • This expanded distribution underscores Cardinal Health’s role as a key conduit for specialty and critical care therapies within U.S. hospital and health system supply chains.
  • We’ll now examine how this enhanced specialty drug distribution, alongside expectations for ongoing operational momentum, shapes Cardinal Health’s investment narrative.

Trump's oil boom is here - pipelines are primed to profit. Discover the 22 US stocks riding the wave.

Advertisement

What Is Cardinal Health's Investment Narrative?

For Cardinal Health, the big-picture belief is that its massive distribution footprint and specialty focus can keep translating into steady, if unspectacular, growth and cash returns, even with a relatively full valuation. The Rapiblyk distribution news fits that story neatly: it reinforces Cardinal Health’s relevance in critical care channels, but on its own it is unlikely to move the needle against the company’s US$234,310.0 million revenue base. Near term, the more important catalysts still look like execution in specialty therapeutics, efficiency gains from automation, and disciplined capital allocation through buybacks and dividends. On the risk side, investors have to stay comfortable with high leverage, thin profit margins and a price to earnings multiple above both industry and peer averages. The Rapiblyk deal adds incremental support to the momentum narrative rather than redefining it.

But there is one financial pressure point in particular that investors should not overlook. Despite retreating, Cardinal Health's shares might still be trading above their fair value and there could be some more downside. Discover how much.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Four Simply Wall St Community fair value views span roughly US$168 to over US$463 per share, underlining how differently people assess Cardinal Health’s prospects. Against that backdrop, concerns about high debt and slim margins may matter more than any single drug distribution win, especially if operational momentum ever slows.

data.title: Assessing Glaukos (GKOS) Valuation After Strong Revenue Update And Epioxa FDA Approval
data.description:
Advertisement

Why Glaukos Is Back on Investors’ Radar

Glaukos (GKOS) has attracted fresh attention after issuing preliminary fourth quarter and full year 2025 revenue figures, reaffirming its 2026 sales outlook, and securing FDA approval for its Epioxa corneal health product.

See our latest analysis for Glaukos.

After a sharp 58.5% 90 day share price return and a 3.2% 30 day share price gain, Glaukos has pulled back 4.7% over the last day. Its 1 year total shareholder return of a 25.1% loss contrasts with a 3 year total shareholder return of a 149.8% gain, suggesting that recent enthusiasm around iDose TR, Epioxa and the updated guidance is now being weighed against execution risks and past volatility.

If Glaukos has caught your eye, it could be a good moment to see what else is happening across ophthalmology and beyond by scanning healthcare stocks for other potential ideas.

With the share price up sharply over 90 days, a value score of 2, a reported intrinsic discount of 56.7% and a net loss of US$87.6 million, you have to ask: is this a buying opportunity, or is the market already pricing in future growth?

Most Popular Narrative: 5.8% Undervalued

Glaukos closed at $119.98, compared with a widely followed fair value narrative of about $127.31, which leans on strong growth and margin assumptions.

Strong ongoing adoption and utilization of iDose TR, a first of its kind procedural pharmaceutical with a unique profile, suggests the early stages of a paradigm shift toward interventional glaucoma therapies, a substantial long term opportunity given the aging population and rising prevalence of glaucoma, likely driving robust multi year revenue and market expansion.

Read the complete narrative.

Curious what kind of revenue lift and margin shift would need to line up with that story, and what sort of earnings multiple ties it all together? The narrative is based on a tight set of growth, profitability, and valuation assumptions that are anything but conservative.

Result: Fair Value of $127.31 (UNDERVALUED)

Have a read of the narrative in full and understand what's behind the forecasts.

However, that story can quickly change if payer reimbursement tightens for key procedures or if clinical or regulatory setbacks slow iDose TR or Epioxa uptake.

Find out about the key risks to this Glaukos narrative.

Another View: High Sales Multiple Raises the Bar

That 5.8% gap to the $127.31 fair value comes with a catch. Glaukos trades on a P/S of 14.7x, compared with 5.7x for peers and 3.3x for the wider US Medical Equipment group. Our fair ratio sits nearer 7.6x, which points to higher valuation risk if growth expectations soften.

data.title: Assessing Viatris (VTRS) Valuation After Inpefa’s First International Launch In The UAE
data.description:

See our latest analysis for Viatris.

The Inpefa launch lands at a time when Viatris has seen a 90 day share price return of 26.78% and a 1 year total shareholder return of 23.94%. This suggests recent momentum after a mixed longer term record, including a 5 year total shareholder return of a 4.88% loss.

If this kind of healthcare news has your attention, it could be a good moment to see what else is on the move across healthcare stocks.

data.title: Columbia Financial (CLBK) Valuation After Strong Earnings Beat And Revenue Growth Surprise
data.description:
Columbia Financial (CLBK) recently posted quarterly results that surprised investors, with revenue up 29.4% year on year and earnings per share ahead of consensus, and the stock rising 15.9% following the release.

See our latest analysis for Columbia Financial.

That strong earnings reaction sits against a more muted backdrop, with a 90 day share price return of 3.38% and a 3 year total shareholder return decline of 20.96%, so recent momentum is building from a weaker long term base.

If you are reassessing your bank holdings after Columbia Financial's surprise quarter, it could be a good moment to broaden your search and check out fast growing stocks with high insider ownership.

With revenue and net income both growing on an annual basis and the share price still only modestly higher over 90 days, the key question now is whether Columbia Financial is undervalued or whether the market is already pricing in future growth.

Advertisement

Preferred P/E of 112.2x: Is it justified?

Columbia Financial trades on a P/E of 112.2x, which stands out against both its own recent earnings profile and typical valuations in the US banking sector.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share and, for banks, it often reflects how the market is weighing earnings quality, growth expectations, and risk. A higher P/E usually suggests investors are willing to pay more for each dollar of earnings, often when they see strong growth ahead or view those earnings as particularly resilient.

In Columbia Financial's case, the current P/E of 112.2x is much higher than the estimated fair P/E of 22.4x. This indicates that the market price embeds a far richer earnings multiple than that fair level implies. That is a wide gap and it suggests the share price is assigning a premium well above the level the fair ratio points to as a potential anchor over time.

The contrast becomes even sharper when you compare Columbia Financial's P/E of 112.2x with the US Banks industry average of 11.8x and the peer average of 16.6x. On both counts, Columbia Financial appears far more expensive than sector and peer benchmarks, while also sitting well above the fair P/E ratio that regression analysis indicates the market could move toward.

Explore the SWS fair ratio for Columbia Financial

Result: Price-to-earnings of 112.2x (OVERVALUED)

However, there are risks, including a very high P/E of 112.2x and reliance on continued annual revenue and net income growth to support that premium.

Find out about the key risks to this Columbia Financial narrative.

data.title: Inari Amertron Berhad (KLSE:INARI) Stock's On A Decline: Are Poor Fundamentals The Cause?
data.description:
With its stock down 31% over the past three months, it is easy to disregard Inari Amertron Berhad (KLSE:INARI). Given that stock prices are usually driven by a company’s fundamentals over the long term, which in this case look pretty weak, we decided to study the company's key financial indicators. In this article, we decided to focus on Inari Amertron Berhad's ROE.

Return on Equity or ROE is a test of how effectively a company is growing its value and managing investors’ money. In other words, it is a profitability ratio which measures the rate of return on the capital provided by the company's shareholders.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

How Do You Calculate Return On Equity?

The formula for return on equity is:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for Inari Amertron Berhad is:

7.8% = RM241m ÷ RM3.1b (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' is the amount earned after tax over the last twelve months. So, this means that for every MYR1 of its shareholder's investments, the company generates a profit of MYR0.08.

View our latest analysis for Inari Amertron Berhad

What Is The Relationship Between ROE And Earnings Growth?

So far, we've learned that ROE is a measure of a company's profitability. Based on how much of its profits the company chooses to reinvest or "retain", we are then able to evaluate a company's future ability to generate profits. Generally speaking, other things being equal, firms with a high return on equity and profit retention, have a higher growth rate than firms that don’t share these attributes.

Inari Amertron Berhad's Earnings Growth And 7.8% ROE

When you first look at it, Inari Amertron Berhad's ROE doesn't look that attractive. However, its ROE is similar to the industry average of 8.1%, so we won't completely dismiss the company. But Inari Amertron Berhad saw a five year net income decline of 4.3% over the past five years. Bear in mind, the company does have a slightly low ROE. Hence, this goes some way in explaining the shrinking earnings.

Next, we compared Inari Amertron Berhad's performance against the industry and found that the industry shrunk its earnings at 5.6% in the same period, which suggests that the company's earnings have been shrinking at a slower rate than its industry, This does offer shareholders some relief

The basis for attaching value to a company is, to a great extent, tied to its earnings growth. What investors need to determine next is if the expected earnings growth, or the lack of it, is already built into the share price. By doing so, they will have an idea if the stock is headed into clear blue waters or if swampy waters await. If you're wondering about Inari Amertron Berhad's's valuation, check out this gauge of its price-to-earnings ratio, as compared to its industry.

Is Inari Amertron Berhad Using Its Retained Earnings Effectively?

Inari Amertron Berhad's declining earnings is not surprising given how the company is spending most of its profits in paying dividends, judging by its three-year median payout ratio of 96% (or a retention ratio of 3.9%). The business is only left with a small pool of capital to reinvest - A vicious cycle that doesn't benefit the company in the long-run.

Additionally, Inari Amertron Berhad has paid dividends over a period of at least ten years, which means that the company's management is determined to pay dividends even if it means little to no earnings growth. Based on the latest analysts' estimates, we found that the company's future payout ratio over the next three years is expected to hold steady at 86%. However, Inari Amertron Berhad's ROE is predicted to rise to 12% despite there being no anticipated change in its payout ratio.

Conclusion

In total, we would have a hard think before deciding on any investment action concerning Inari Amertron Berhad. Particularly, its ROE is a huge disappointment, not to mention its lack of proper reinvestment into the business. As a result its earnings growth has also been quite disappointing. That being so, the latest industry analyst forecasts show that the analysts are expecting to see a huge improvement in the company's earnings growth rate. To know more about the latest analysts predictions for the company, check out this visualization of analyst forecasts for the company.

data.title: Avenue Supermarts (NSE:DMART) Is Experiencing Growth In Returns On Capital
data.description:
There are a few key trends to look for if we want to identify the next multi-bagger. Typically, we'll want to notice a trend of growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and alongside that, an expanding base of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. So when we looked at Avenue Supermarts (NSE:DMART) and its trend of ROCE, we really liked what we saw.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Return On Capital Employed (ROCE): What Is It?

For those who don't know, ROCE is a measure of a company's yearly pre-tax profit (its return), relative to the capital employed in the business. To calculate this metric for Avenue Supermarts, this is the formula:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.16 = ₹39b ÷ (₹269b - ₹28b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to December 2025).

Thus, Avenue Supermarts has an ROCE of 16%. In absolute terms, that's a satisfactory return, but compared to the Consumer Retailing industry average of 10% it's much better.

Check out our latest analysis for Avenue Supermarts

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Avenue Supermarts compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you'd like, you can check out the forecasts from the analysts covering Avenue Supermarts for free.

What Does the ROCE Trend For Avenue Supermarts Tell Us?

Investors would be pleased with what's happening at Avenue Supermarts. The data shows that returns on capital have increased substantially over the last five years to 16%. Basically the business is earning more per dollar of capital invested and in addition to that, 108% more capital is being employed now too. This can indicate that there's plenty of opportunities to invest capital internally and at ever higher rates, a combination that's common among multi-baggers.

In Conclusion...

In summary, it's great to see that Avenue Supermarts can compound returns by consistently reinvesting capital at increasing rates of return, because these are some of the key ingredients of those highly sought after multi-baggers. Since the stock has only returned 38% to shareholders over the last five years, the promising fundamentals may not be recognized yet by investors. So exploring more about this stock could uncover a good opportunity, if the valuation and other metrics stack up.

Before jumping to any conclusions though, we need to know what value we're getting for the current share price. That's where you can check out our FREE intrinsic value estimation for DMART that compares the share price and estimated value.

While Avenue Supermarts may not currently earn the highest returns, we've compiled a list of companies that currently earn more than 25% return on equity. Check out this free list here.

data.title: Is Scanfil Oyj (HEL:SCANFL) Using Too Much Debt?
data.description:
Howard Marks put it nicely when he said that, rather than worrying about share price volatility, 'The possibility of permanent loss is the risk I worry about... and every practical investor I know worries about.' So it might be obvious that you need to consider debt, when you think about how risky any given stock is, because too much debt can sink a company. We can see that Scanfil Oyj (HEL:SCANFL) does use debt in its business. But the real question is whether this debt is making the company risky.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Debt is a tool to help businesses grow, but if a business is incapable of paying off its lenders, then it exists at their mercy. In the worst case scenario, a company can go bankrupt if it cannot pay its creditors. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Of course, plenty of companies use debt to fund growth, without any negative consequences. When we think about a company's use of debt, we first look at cash and debt together.

What Is Scanfil Oyj's Net Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that Scanfil Oyj had debt of €38.4m at the end of September 2025, a reduction from €41.9m over a year. But it also has €56.0m in cash to offset that, meaning it has €17.6m net cash.

How Healthy Is Scanfil Oyj's Balance Sheet?

We can see from the most recent balance sheet that Scanfil Oyj had liabilities of €207.6m falling due within a year, and liabilities of €46.1m due beyond that. On the other hand, it had cash of €56.0m and €180.5m worth of receivables due within a year. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by €17.2m.

Since publicly traded Scanfil Oyj shares are worth a total of €694.9m, it seems unlikely that this level of liabilities would be a major threat. But there are sufficient liabilities that we would certainly recommend shareholders continue to monitor the balance sheet, going forward. While it does have liabilities worth noting, Scanfil Oyj also has more cash than debt, so we're pretty confident it can manage its debt safely.

View our latest analysis for Scanfil Oyj

Scanfil Oyj's EBIT was pretty flat over the last year, but that shouldn't be an issue given the it doesn't have a lot of debt. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But ultimately the future profitability of the business will decide if Scanfil Oyj can strengthen its balance sheet over time. So if you want to see what the professionals think, you might find this free report on analyst profit forecasts to be interesting.

Finally, a business needs free cash flow to pay off debt; accounting profits just don't cut it. While Scanfil Oyj has net cash on its balance sheet, it's still worth taking a look at its ability to convert earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) to free cash flow, to help us understand how quickly it is building (or eroding) that cash balance. Happily for any shareholders, Scanfil Oyj actually produced more free cash flow than EBIT over the last three years. That sort of strong cash generation warms our hearts like a puppy in a bumblebee suit.

Summing Up

We could understand if investors are concerned about Scanfil Oyj's liabilities, but we can be reassured by the fact it has has net cash of €17.6m. And it impressed us with free cash flow of €51m, being 100% of its EBIT. So is Scanfil Oyj's debt a risk? It doesn't seem so to us. Over time, share prices tend to follow earnings per share, so if you're interested in Scanfil Oyj, you may well want to click here to check an interactive graph of its earnings per share history.

If, after all that, you're more interested in a fast growing company with a rock-solid balance sheet, then check out our list of net cash growth stocks without delay.

data.title: While institutions invested in Hankook Tire & Technology Co., Ltd. (KRX:161390) benefited from last week's 8.4% gain, public companies stood to gain the most
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • The considerable ownership by public companies in Hankook Tire & Technology indicates that they collectively have a greater say in management and business strategy
  • The top 4 shareholders own 53% of the company
  • Insiders own 13% of Hankook Tire & Technology

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Every investor in Hankook Tire & Technology Co., Ltd. (KRX:161390) should be aware of the most powerful shareholder groups. We can see that public companies own the lion's share in the company with 31% ownership. That is, the group stands to benefit the most if the stock rises (or lose the most if there is a downturn).

While public companies were the group that benefitted the most from last week’s ₩634b market cap gain, institutions too had a 30% share in those profits.

In the chart below, we zoom in on the different ownership groups of Hankook Tire & Technology.

View our latest analysis for Hankook Tire & Technology

What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Hankook Tire & Technology?

Institutional investors commonly compare their own returns to the returns of a commonly followed index. So they generally do consider buying larger companies that are included in the relevant benchmark index.

As you can see, institutional investors have a fair amount of stake in Hankook Tire & Technology. This suggests some credibility amongst professional investors. But we can't rely on that fact alone since institutions make bad investments sometimes, just like everyone does. When multiple institutions own a stock, there's always a risk that they are in a 'crowded trade'. When such a trade goes wrong, multiple parties may compete to sell stock fast. This risk is higher in a company without a history of growth. You can see Hankook Tire & Technology's historic earnings and revenue below, but keep in mind there's always more to the story.

We note that hedge funds don't have a meaningful investment in Hankook Tire & Technology. Hankook & Company Co., Ltd. is currently the company's largest shareholder with 31% of shares outstanding. Hyun-Bum Cho is the second largest shareholder owning 7.9% of common stock, and National Pension Service holds about 7.8% of the company stock.

Our research also brought to light the fact that roughly 53% of the company is controlled by the top 4 shareholders suggesting that these owners wield significant influence on the business.

While studying institutional ownership for a company can add value to your research, it is also a good practice to research analyst recommendations to get a deeper understand of a stock's expected performance. There are plenty of analysts covering the stock, so it might be worth seeing what they are forecasting, too.

Insider Ownership Of Hankook Tire & Technology

The definition of company insiders can be subjective and does vary between jurisdictions. Our data reflects individual insiders, capturing board members at the very least. The company management answer to the board and the latter should represent the interests of shareholders. Notably, sometimes top-level managers are on the board themselves.

Most consider insider ownership a positive because it can indicate the board is well aligned with other shareholders. However, on some occasions too much power is concentrated within this group.

Our most recent data indicates that insiders own a reasonable proportion of Hankook Tire & Technology Co., Ltd.. Insiders own ₩1.0t worth of shares in the ₩8.2t company. That's quite meaningful. It is good to see this level of investment. You can check here to see if those insiders have been buying recently.

General Public Ownership

The general public, who are usually individual investors, hold a 26% stake in Hankook Tire & Technology. While this group can't necessarily call the shots, it can certainly have a real influence on how the company is run.

Public Company Ownership

Public companies currently own 31% of Hankook Tire & Technology stock. We can't be certain but it is quite possible this is a strategic stake. The businesses may be similar, or work together.

Next Steps:

I find it very interesting to look at who exactly owns a company. But to truly gain insight, we need to consider other information, too. Case in point: We've spotted 2 warning signs for Hankook Tire & Technology you should be aware of.

Ultimately the future is most important. You can access this free report on analyst forecasts for the company.

NB: Figures in this article are calculated using data from the last twelve months, which refer to the 12-month period ending on the last date of the month the financial statement is dated. This may not be consistent with full year annual report figures.

data.title: The Returns At Hyundai Autoever (KRX:307950) Aren't Growing
data.description:
Finding a business that has the potential to grow substantially is not easy, but it is possible if we look at a few key financial metrics. One common approach is to try and find a company with returns on capital employed (ROCE) that are increasing, in conjunction with a growing amount of capital employed. Ultimately, this demonstrates that it's a business that is reinvesting profits at increasing rates of return. With that in mind, the ROCE of Hyundai Autoever (KRX:307950) looks decent, right now, so lets see what the trend of returns can tell us.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

Just to clarify if you're unsure, ROCE is a metric for evaluating how much pre-tax income (in percentage terms) a company earns on the capital invested in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Hyundai Autoever:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.12 = ₩251b ÷ (₩3.3t - ₩1.1t) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Thus, Hyundai Autoever has an ROCE of 12%. In absolute terms, that's a satisfactory return, but compared to the IT industry average of 9.0% it's much better.

View our latest analysis for Hyundai Autoever

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Hyundai Autoever compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you'd like to see what analysts are forecasting going forward, you should check out our free analyst report for Hyundai Autoever .

The Trend Of ROCE

While the current returns on capital are decent, they haven't changed much. The company has employed 239% more capital in the last five years, and the returns on that capital have remained stable at 12%. Since 12% is a moderate ROCE though, it's good to see a business can continue to reinvest at these decent rates of return. Over long periods of time, returns like these might not be too exciting, but with consistency they can pay off in terms of share price returns.

What We Can Learn From Hyundai Autoever's ROCE

To sum it up, Hyundai Autoever has simply been reinvesting capital steadily, at those decent rates of return. And the stock has done incredibly well with a 280% return over the last five years, so long term investors are no doubt ecstatic with that result. So even though the stock might be more "expensive" than it was before, we think the strong fundamentals warrant this stock for further research.

One more thing, we've spotted 1 warning sign facing Hyundai Autoever that you might find interesting.

While Hyundai Autoever isn't earning the highest return, check out this free list of companies that are earning high returns on equity with solid balance sheets.

data.title: Dividend Aristocrats Inclusion And Record-Seeking 2026 Plan Might Change The Case For Investing In Mullen Group (TSX:MTL)
data.description:
• Mullen Group Ltd. recently declared a monthly dividend of C$0.07 per common share, payable on February 17, 2026 to shareholders of record on January 31, 2026, and outlined 2025–2026 revenue guidance alongside an approved 2026 business plan targeting record performance.
  • The company’s upcoming inclusion in the S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Index underscores its established dividend track record and reinforces its income-oriented appeal for investors.
  • We’ll now examine how Mullen Group’s Dividend Aristocrats inclusion and record-focused 2026 business plan shape its broader investment narrative.

These 9 companies survived and thrived after COVID and have the right ingredients to survive Trump's tariffs. Discover why before your portfolio feels the trade war pinch.

Advertisement

What Is Mullen Group's Investment Narrative?

For Mullen Group, the big-picture belief is that a steady, acquisition-supported trucking and logistics platform can translate into durable cash generation, even when freight markets are merely “okay” rather than strong. The reaffirmed C$0.07 monthly dividend and upcoming Dividend Aristocrats inclusion fit neatly into that story, reinforcing the income angle and potentially broadening the shareholder base, but they do not radically change the near term catalysts. Those still hinge on execution against the C$2.1 billion 2025 and C$2.3–2.4 billion 2026 revenue targets, integration of recent acquisitions and any surprise in the upcoming Q4 2025 results. On the risk side, elevated debt, thinner profit margins and a freight backdrop that management itself calls challenging remain front and center, even with the “record year” 2026 business plan on the table.

However, one risk in particular could matter more than income-focused investors might expect. Mullen Group's shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued by 18%. Find out what it's worth.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Three Simply Wall St Community fair value views for Mullen Group range from C$15.00 to about C$20.44, showing wide differences in what investors think the shares are worth. Set those opinions against the company’s record-focused 2026 plan and the reliance on acquisitions in a still-tough freight market, and you can see why some investors may weigh income visibility against execution and balance sheet risks very differently.

data.title: Will Royalty Pharma's (RPRX) Dividend Hike and Guidance Reset Change Its Efficiency and Growth Narrative
data.description:
• Royalty Pharma, which acquires rights to portions of biopharmaceutical sales instead of conducting its own research, recently raised its dividend to US$0.235 payable in March 2026 and issued higher guidance ahead of its fourth quarter and full-year 2025 results scheduled for February 11, 2026.
  • This comes after two years of flat sales and a 4.4 percentage point drop in adjusted operating margin over five years, highlighting the tension between investor optimism and questions about how the company can improve efficiency and growth, especially amid concerns about low trading liquidity.
  • With the dividend increase signaling management’s capital-allocation priorities, we now examine how these developments shape Royalty Pharma’s broader investment narrative.

Outshine the giants: these 23 early-stage AI stocks could fund your retirement.

Advertisement

What Is Royalty Pharma's Investment Narrative?

To own Royalty Pharma, you have to be comfortable with a business that swaps drug development risk for exposure to long-lived royalty streams, and accept that growth depends on disciplined capital deployment rather than blockbuster discoveries. The recent uplift in guidance and the dividend increase to US$0.235 reinforce that management is leaning into a cash-return story even as sales have been broadly flat and margins have slipped over time. In the near term, the key catalyst is whether the February 11 results and outlook translate that optimism into clearer evidence of improving efficiency and a healthier earnings trajectory. At the same time, higher payouts and ongoing buybacks could amplify existing concerns about balance sheet leverage and relatively low trading liquidity, which may become more important if sentiment turns.

However, one risk in particular could catch income-focused investors off guard. Royalty Pharma's shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued. Find out how large the opportunity might be.

Exploring Other Perspectives

The five fair value estimates from the Simply Wall St Community stretch from US$39.10 to a very large US$179.83, underlining how far apart individual views can be. Set against recent guidance and margin pressure, that spread invites you to weigh how much execution risk and liquidity constraint you think the current price already reflects.

data.title: Assessing Utz Brands (UTZ) Valuation After Recent Share Price Weakness And Margin Improvement Hopes
data.description:
Advertisement

Recent performance snapshot

Utz Brands (UTZ) has been under pressure recently, with the stock showing a negative return over the past 3 months and over the past year, while year to date performance is slightly positive.

See our latest analysis for Utz Brands.

With the share price at $10.42, Utz Brands has seen momentum fade, with a 90 day share price return of around a 15% decline and a 1 year total shareholder return of about a 20% decline, pointing to weaker sentiment despite a slightly positive year to date share price return.

If recent moves in Utz have you reassessing your watchlist, this could be a good moment to broaden your search and check out fast growing stocks with high insider ownership.

With the share price at $10.42, a value score of 4, and the stock trading at a sizeable discount to the average analyst price target, the key question is whether Utz is a bargain or if the market already reflects its future growth.

Most Popular Narrative: 32.1% Undervalued

With Utz Brands closing at $10.42 against a narrative fair value of $15.35, the most followed view in the market sees meaningful upside still on the table, anchored to detailed assumptions about revenue, margins, and future earnings power that differ from current pricing.

Significant supply chain optimization, including automation, plant consolidation, and productivity initiatives, is leading to sustained gross margin expansion (~6% productivity improvement). Management is guiding to further margin improvements in the latter half of the year and into 2026, positively impacting EBITDA and net earnings.

Read the complete narrative.

Want to see how modest revenue growth, a lift in profit margins, and a future earnings multiple all fit together into that $15.35 fair value? The narrative connects a full earnings ramp, higher profitability, and a specific discount rate into one coherent pricing case, but the exact mix of assumptions is where things get interesting.

Result: Fair Value of $15.35 (UNDERVALUED)

Have a read of the narrative in full and understand what's behind the forecasts.

However, the story could change quickly if westward expansion fails to deliver enough sales to justify the higher costs, or if private labels and healthier rivals put pressure on Utz's pricing power.
